title: Mixed Strategies Arise Only When a Player is Indifferent

lines.text: Chapter 2, section 5. Mixed strategies arise only when a player is indifferent.
You might have heard that good players randomize their actions — sometimes calling, sometimes folding with the same hand.
That sounds mysterious, but the logic is actually pretty clean.
You only need to randomize when two options return exactly the same expected value.
If calling is clearly better than folding, just call every time — no mixing needed.
If folding is clearly better, just fold.
Randomizing when one option dominates the other is just leaving money on the table.
The only time mixing makes mathematical sense is at the exact point of indifference, where both choices earn the same amount on average.
In the clairvoyance game, Ivan reaches that point when Opal is bluffing at precisely the right frequency.
If Opal over-bluffs, calling becomes strictly better for Ivan and he should always call.
If Opal under-bluffs, folding becomes strictly better and Ivan should always fold.
Randomizing is not a trick — it's a signal that your opponent has done their job perfectly.
